name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
summary: The Russian Civil War was a multi-sided conflict after the 1917 revolutions involving Bolshevik forces, White armies, Ukrainian and other nationalist forces, peasant insurgents, foreign interventionists, and local warlords. Total deaths are usually placed around 7–12 million, overwhelmingly from famine, disease, and civilian mass violence rather than battlefield deaths alone. Attribution is strongest for military losses, Red/White terror episodes, and anti-Jewish pogroms, but the millions of famine and epidemic deaths cannot be cleanly assigned to a single belligerent.

military_killed.notes: Low combines Urlanis's 125,000 Red Army KIA with Patterson's cited Soviet-demographic estimate of about 100,000 Red Army typhus deaths in 1918–1920. High is the broader Red Army dead-or-missing figure reported in military-loss summaries and is not directly comparable to KIA-only estimates.

breakdown.cause: Red Terror executions, Cheka killings, revolutionary tribunals, hostage/prison shootings
breakdown.victims_group: Suspected counterrevolutionaries, hostages, bourgeois civilians, political opponents, prisoners
breakdown.notes: Conservative structured range based on documented Cheka/tribunal execution estimates and Ronald Suny's summarized Red Terror range. Broader estimates up to 1.2–2 million exist but mix executions, custody deaths, pacification, and wider repression.
Derived from Ronald Suny's summarized attribution of 8.5% of 35,000–150,000 Jewish pogrom deaths to Bolshevik forces; recorded incident counts list 106 Red Army pogroms/excesses out of 1,236.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The total adds conservative Red Terror estimates to the Suny-derived Red pogrom estimate. This avoids using very high broad 'Red Terror' estimates that may include indirect deaths or overlap with famine and pacification campaigns.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilian population in famine regions, especially Volga, Ukraine, Urals, and surrounding areas
deaths_from_actions.notes: Total 1921–1922 famine mortality, not a Bolshevik-only toll. Soviet requisitioning and War Communism are identified as contributing factors alongside drought, prior war destruction, transport collapse, livestock loss, epidemics, and Allied blockade.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Tambov peasants, insurgents, hostages, families of rebels
deaths_from_actions.notes: Estimates vary by definition. Some summaries cite about 15,000 killed during suppression; Sennikov's broader estimate gives about 240,000 total Tambov regional losses from war, executions, and imprisonment in concentration camps in 1920–1922. Not added mechanically to Red Terror or famine totals because of likely overlap.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Cossacks, especially Don and Kuban Cossacks
deaths_from_actions.notes: Decossackization estimates are highly contentious and often mix deaths with deportations; this range should be read as killed-or-deported rather than confirmed deaths alone.

military_killed.notes: Low is Urlanis's KIA estimate for White armies and Poles combined, so it is not cleanly White-only. High is the broad 650,000 White Army killed figure reported in secondary military-loss summaries.

breakdown.cause: White Terror executions, punitive actions, imprisonment killings
breakdown.victims_group: Bolsheviks, socialists, suspected sympathizers, prisoners, civilians in White-controlled territories
breakdown.notes: Beyrau estimates 20,000–100,000 victims excluding pogroms; Erlikhman gives 300,000; Ratkovsky argues the number may exceed 500,000 excluding pogroms. The high is therefore a rounded upper estimate rather than a consensus.
Derived from Ronald Suny's summarized attribution of 17% of 35,000–150,000 Jewish pogrom deaths to White armies; recorded incident counts list 213 Denikin-army pogroms/excesses out of 1,236.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The structured range combines White Terror estimates excluding pogroms with the Suny-derived White pogrom share. White Terror figures are among the most contested numbers in the conflict.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians in White-held or contested regions
deaths_from_actions.notes: White armies and White-controlled regimes contributed to displacement, transport disruption, requisitions, imprisonment, and epidemic conditions, but no reliable White-only indirect-death toll was found. Direct terror and pogrom deaths are counted above.

sides.name: Ukrainian nationalist, Green, peasant-insurgent, and local warlord forces
members: local peasant bands and deserter formations

military_killed.notes: No reliable aggregate military-death estimate separated these heterogeneous forces from Red, White, Polish, and civilian losses.

breakdown.notes: Derived from Ronald Suny's summarized attribution of 40% of 35,000–150,000 Jewish pogrom deaths to Ukrainians under Symon Petliura; recorded incident counts list 493 UPR/Petliura pogroms/excesses.

Derived from Ronald Suny's summarized attribution of 25% of 35,000–150,000 Jewish pogrom deaths to other Ukrainian forces; recorded incident counts list 307 pogroms/excesses by independent Ukrainian warlords.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: This side's most clearly attributable mass-casualty category is anti-Jewish pogrom killing. It excludes non-Jewish civilian killings for which no aggregate separated estimate was found.

military_killed.notes: No reliable aggregate military-death estimate was found for all foreign and border-state intervention forces. Urlanis's 175,500 KIA estimate combines White armies and Poles and cannot be used as a separate foreign total.

breakdown.notes: Recorded incident counts list 32 Polish Army pogroms/excesses out of 1,236, but no reliable separate fatality count was found.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Foreign and border-state forces committed local violence, but I found no responsible aggregate direct-civilian death estimate separated from other belligerents.

deaths_from_actions.notes: The Allied economic blockade is identified as a contributing factor to the 1921–1922 famine, whose total mortality is commonly cited around 5 million and ranged in estimates from 1–10 million. No blockade-only death toll was found.

totals.notes: Military_low uses Urlanis's 300,000 KIA plus 450,000 military disease deaths for both sides. Military_high combines broad Red dead/missing and White killed figures and excludes unseparated nationalist and foreign forces. Civilian_low and civilian_high are residuals from the 7–12 million overall range and should not be treated as independently measured civilian totals. Some summaries allow a high-end total up to 14 million.

key_atrocities.name: Russian famine of 1921–1922
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Multi-causal; Soviet requisitioning/War Communism and Allied blockade contributed alongside drought and war destruction
key_atrocities.victims_group: Civilian population in Volga, Ukraine, Urals, and other famine regions
key_atrocities.notes: Most frequently quoted point estimate is about 5 million deaths; not cleanly attributable to one belligerent.

key_atrocities.name: Anti-Jewish pogroms in Ukraine and Belarus
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Multiple: Ukrainian nationalist forces, White armies, Green/local warlords, Red Army units, Polish forces
key_atrocities.notes: Werth gives about 150,000 victims, including 125,000 in Ukraine and 25,000 in Belarus; Suny's summarized range is 35,000–150,000 deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Proskurov pogrom
key_atrocities.notes: Werth lists around 1,500 victims on 15–17 February 1919.

key_atrocities.name: Fastov pogrom
key_atrocities.notes: Werth lists 1,000–1,500 victims in September 1919; another summary says over 1,500 Jews were murdered in Fastov by Denikin's Volunteer Army.

key_atrocities.name: Early Red Terror
key_atrocities.victims_group: Hostages, suspected counterrevolutionaries, bourgeois civilians, political opponents
key_atrocities.notes: Werth estimates 10,000–15,000 victims in September–October 1918 alone.

key_atrocities.name: Tambov suppression
key_atrocities.victims_group: Tambov peasants, insurgents, hostages, families of rebels
key_atrocities.notes: Lower figure reflects killed during suppression in some summaries; higher figure reflects Sennikov's broader total losses from war, executions, and imprisonment in camps.

uncertainty_notes: The largest uncertainty is attribution rather than the existence of mass death. Battlefield and military disease estimates are not directly comparable across sources; Red and White Terror estimates depend heavily on whether only documented executions or broader repression/custody deaths are counted; pogrom attribution is relatively better documented but still imperfect; famine and epidemic deaths cannot be cleanly apportioned among natural causes, Bolshevik requisitioning and War Communism, White and other military disruption, transport collapse, displacement, and Allied blockade. Per-side figures therefore should not be summed mechanically to produce the grand total.
